Q1: My study routine was honestly all over the place because I couldn't rely on being on campus much. I'd usually block out time late at night or early morning when the house was quiet and I could focus. I downloaded everything I could when I had good internet access, then worked through materials offline. I kept handwritten notes in a spiral notebook that became my lifeline. With limited resources, I had to be strategic about when and how I studied, so I made every session count.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot based on what was due and what was happening at home. Some weeks I could stick to a rough schedule, but other times family responsibilities or work would throw everything off. I learned to be flexible and just grab study time whenever I could find it. Deadlines definitely pushed me to focus harder, but I tried to stay ahead when possible because I knew unexpected things could come up. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work.
Q3: I usually logged in when I needed to check assignment requirements or deadlines, or when I knew new materials had been posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to download things quickly before my internet got spotty. Honestly, I didn't browse around much—I went in with a purpose, got what I needed, and got out. The peer support group would sometimes remind me to check certain things, which helped keep me on track.
Q4: Not really a daily routine, but I did develop a pattern of checking in at the start of each week to see what was coming. It became almost automatic to do a quick download session on Sundays when I had reliable wifi at my aunt's place. The LMS itself never felt like home base for me—it was more like a supply station where I'd stock up before doing the real work elsewhere. My routine was built around working offline, not around the platform.
Q5: The reading materials and lecture notes were absolutely essential for me because I could download them and study anywhere. I also found the discussion boards helpful when I could access them—connecting with other students helped me understand concepts I was struggling with. The assignment rubrics were gold because they told me exactly what was expected. I appreciated anything that was clearly organized and downloadable because of my situation with internet access.
Q6: I barely touched the videos because they ate up too much data and took forever to buffer. I know there were almost 300 minutes of video content I was supposed to watch, but I just couldn't make it work with my internet situation. I'd try to find transcripts or summaries instead, or ask peers what the key points were. Some of the interactive features I also skipped because they required constant connectivity. It wasn't that I didn't value them—I just literally couldn't access them reliably.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what I'd be assessed on or if it genuinely helped me understand something difficult. I needed to see the purpose clearly because I couldn't afford to waste limited study time on things that felt like busy work. If I could tell it would improve my assignment or exam performance, I was all in. The peer discussions were worth it too because they made me feel less alone in this journey.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I usually couldn't afford time-wise. With required work, I knew exactly what I had to do and by when, so I could plan around my constraints. Optional stuff, even if interesting, often got pushed aside because I had to prioritize what would actually count toward my grade. Sometimes I felt sad about missing out on enrichment activities, but survival mode meant focusing on essentials.
Q9: Almost all of my studying happened outside the LMS, honestly. I'd download PDFs, print them when I could afford to, and work from physical copies with highlighters and sticky notes. I borrowed textbooks from the library and took extensive handwritten notes. I also used free YouTube videos and other websites when I had internet access. My notebook full of notes probably represented more learning than anything the system could track.
Q10: There were countless hours where I was deeply engaged with course material but completely invisible to the LMS. I'd spend entire evenings working through problems in my notebook, re-reading printed lecture notes, or discussing concepts with my peer support group in person or over WhatsApp. Sometimes I'd access a document once, download it, and then study it intensively for days without ever logging back in. My best learning happened away from the screen.
Q11: Absolutely, this was my standard approach. I'd log in, download everything available for that module in one session, then work through it all offline over the following days or weeks. One login might represent hours of subsequent study that never showed up in the system. I got really good at organizing my downloaded files so I could access them without internet. It was the only way I could make this work with my resources.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially when I felt overwhelmed by everything happening outside of school. Sometimes the sadness would hit and I'd struggle to find the energy to engage. When family stress peaked or when I was worried about money, the course felt like just one more weight. But I kept reminding myself why I was doing this—I'm climbing toward something better. My peer support group helped pull me through those darker moments.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from working or dealing with family stuff, I'd log in and just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities meant I sometimes had to choose between studying and other obligations. Fatigue made it hard to engage meaningfully even when I did find time. I learned to recognize when I was too depleted to actually learn and would try to rest instead of wasting time pretending to study.
Q14: Yes, there were times when I'd open materials and scroll through them but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I might be physically looking at the screen but mentally processing worries about bills or family issues. Those sessions felt hollow—I was going through the motions without really learning. I'd usually have to come back later when I was in a better headspace to actually engage with the content properly.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts more than I should have because they required being online and mentally present at the same time, which was hard to coordinate. Sometimes I avoided starting big assignments because the thought of them felt overwhelming when I was already stretched thin. I knew they were important, but my brain would sometimes just shut down as a protection mechanism. I'd eventually push through, but the delay caused extra stress.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my compass for using the LMS. I'd check in more frequently as deadlines approached to make sure I hadn't missed any updates or requirements. They forced me to prioritize and plan my limited online time carefully. Without deadlines, I might have drifted, but they kept me accountable and focused. I learned to work backward from due dates to figure out when I needed to download materials and when I needed to submit.
Q17: Feedback definitely motivated me to keep going, especially when it was positive. Seeing that my hard work was paying off with good scores made the sacrifices feel worthwhile. When I got constructive criticism, I'd really take it to heart and adjust my approach for the next assignment. The feedback validated that my offline study methods were working, even if my engagement looked low on paper. It kept me believing I could succeed.
Q18: The course structure was mostly helpful because it was predictable—I could plan my downloads and study sessions around when new modules dropped. Having clear deadlines and organized modules made it easier to navigate with limited time online. However, some aspects assumed constant connectivity, which was challenging for me. I appreciated when instructors posted materials early so I could work ahead during good internet periods.
Q19: Downloadable resources made everything easier for me—anything I could save and access offline was a blessing. Clear rubrics and expectations also helped because I didn't have to keep logging in to figure out what was required. What made it harder was anything that required real-time participation or streaming, like live sessions or video content. The peer support intervention was actually really helpful because it gave me human connection without requiring constant LMS presence.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for things, trying to understand the system, or feeling lost about where to start. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes you're clicking around trying to find what you need. Others might be dealing with anxiety or perfectionism, preparing extensively but struggling to actually submit. Or maybe they're like me in reverse—their real work happens elsewhere, but for them, the submission step is the barrier.
Q21: This is literally my story. My LMS activity looks low because I do most of my learning offline after downloading materials. I barely watched videos and my login frequency wasn't high, but I worked incredibly hard with my printed notes and notebooks. My exam and assignment scores show that I was deeply engaged with the content—just not through the platform. The LMS only captures one small piece of how I actually learned.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone logged in, what they clicked on, and whether they submitted work on time. They're good at tracking digital footprints and deadline compliance. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement versus just time spent, emotional investment, and learning that happens through peer conversations or personal notes. They see the shadow of engagement, not the substance of it.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to pass. It's about wrestling with concepts until they make sense, connecting ideas to your own life, and pushing through even when it's hard. For me, engagement looked like late nights with my notebook, discussions with peers, and that feeling of satisfaction when something finally clicked. It's internal and personal—not just measurable activity.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much I sacrificed to succeed in this course, or how I built a whole system of offline learning to work around my limitations. It wouldn't capture the emotional journey—the sadness I sometimes felt, the resilience I had to summon, or the support I got from peers that kept me going. It can't measure how meaningful this achievement is for someone climbing from where I started. My grades tell one story, but the human story behind them is invisible to any system.
